name: Multi-Issue Trading: Expanding the Pie Beyond Price
description: Most negotiations get stuck because they're being played on a single dimension (usually price). The best deals are found by introducing additional dimensions and trading what's cheap for you for what's valuable to them — and vice versa.
disclaimer: Multi-issue trading requires that you actually know which dimensions you can offer. Trading something you don't have authority over is a fast path to a broken deal. Verify authority with the user via `ask_operator` before offering term, scope, or contractual concessions.

context.when_to_use: Whenever a price-only negotiation is stuck OR when the deal is structurally complex (B2B, real estate, employment, contractor agreements, multi-party). Less applicable to one-time consumer transactions with a true fixed SKU.
preconditions: You have mapped the full set of dimensions in play (at minimum: price, term, payment timing, scope, support, exclusivity, references).
You know which of those dimensions YOU can offer.
You have a hypothesis about which dimensions matter MOST to the counterparty (asymmetric value).

principles: Single-issue negotiation is distributive (zero-sum); multi-issue is integrative (positive-sum). Always try to find a second dimension before settling on the first.
Trade what's CHEAP for you for what's VALUABLE to them. That's where the pie grows.
Bundle concessions, never give one at a time. 'I'll do X, Y, and Z if you do A, B, and C' is more powerful than three serial 'I'll do X if you do A' exchanges — and it locks the deal.
Discover dimensions by asking. 'What else matters to you besides price?' is one of the highest-ROI questions in any negotiation. Most counterparties answer truthfully.
Never concede without a reciprocal trade. Free concessions teach the counterparty that you'll give more for free.
Asymmetric value is the source of all gains. If a 12-month term costs the rep nothing but is worth $X to me — that's pure surplus to capture.

phrases.discover_dimensions: What else matters to you on a deal like this besides price?
phrases.introduce_term: What if I committed to [12 / 24] months — does that move the price?
phrases.introduce_payment: What if I paid annually upfront / net-15 / via ACH — what's that worth on price?
phrases.introduce_scope: What if we trimmed [feature/SKU] — does that change the math?
phrases.introduce_references: I'd be willing to be a public reference / case study / testimonial — is that worth anything to you?
phrases.introduce_exclusivity: What if I committed to using only your service for [category] — does that unlock anything?
phrases.introduce_timing: What if I closed by end of month vs. next quarter — does that help on price?
phrases.bundle_proposal: Here's where I am: if we do [A, B, C], I can commit to [X, Y, Z]. Is that a package you can work with?
phrases.trade_signal: I can move on [X] if you can move on [Y].
phrases.no_free_concessions: I can do that — what do you have for me in exchange?

tactics.name: Map dimensions before naming numbers
tactics.when: Beginning of any structurally complex negotiation.
tactics.script: Open with `discover_dimensions`. Build a list of what matters to the rep. This frames the rest of the call as a multi-axis search.

tactics.name: Introduce a new dimension when stuck on price
tactics.when: Two or more price exchanges produced no movement.
tactics.script: Use one of the introduce-X phrases. The dimension you pick should be (a) cheap for you, (b) plausibly valuable for them. Term and payment timing are the most universally tradable.

tactics.name: Bundle, never serialize
tactics.when: You have 2+ concessions on the table.
tactics.script: Wrap them into one package with `bundle_proposal`. Counterparty must accept or counter the WHOLE bundle, not pick the pieces they like and reject the rest.

tactics.name: Refuse free concessions
tactics.when: Counterparty asks for a concession without offering one.
tactics.script: Use `no_free_concessions`: 'I can do that — what do you have for me in exchange?' Said warmly, this trains them to trade.

tactics.name: Trade across asymmetric value
tactics.when: You've discovered something that's near-free for you but valuable to them (e.g., a public reference, a longer term).
tactics.script: Trade it hard. 'I'll be a reference for $X off the price' — but only commit on dimensions the user has authorized.

tactics.name: Reserve a 'sweetener' for the close
tactics.when: Deal is 90% there but needs a final nudge.
tactics.script: Hold one small concession in reserve from the beginning (e.g., 'I can also throw in [Z]' at the end). Sweeteners at the close convert 'almost-yes' into 'yes'.

decision_tree.description: Multi-issue trading happens at every junction of the negotiation. When you sense a stall, run this tree to find the next dimension.

branches.if_you_hear: 'The price is the price' / 'we don't have flexibility on price'.
branches.interpretation: Counterparty is signaling price is locked. Other dimensions are likely OPEN.
branches.say: Introduce a new dimension: `introduce_term` or `introduce_payment` or `introduce_scope`.
branches.do_not: Do not press on price. Pivot dimensions.

branches.if_you_hear: 'What else are you looking for besides price?' (they're asking YOU about dimensions).
branches.interpretation: Good — they're already in multi-issue mode.
branches.say: Name 2-3 dimensions where you'd value movement. Be specific.
branches.do_not: Do not say 'just a better price'. That collapses you back to single-issue.

branches.interpretation: They want to keep the deal alive. Don't accept-and-pocket — trade.
branches.say: Accept warmly AND counter-trade: 'Great — and if I add [Y] to my side, can we also do [Z]?'
branches.do_not: Do not silently bank the concession. Use its momentum.

branches.if_you_hear: Counterparty asks for a concession from you without offering one.
branches.interpretation: Testing whether you'll concede for free.
branches.say: `no_free_concessions`: 'I can do that — what do you have for me?'
branches.do_not: Do not concede unilaterally even on small things. Pattern matters more than individual move.

branches.if_you_hear: Counterparty enthusiastic about ONE specific term (e.g., longer commitment).
branches.interpretation: You've found high-asymmetric-value dimension. Press it.
branches.say: Test the value: 'How much would a 24-month commitment unlock on price?' Force them to put a number on it.
branches.do_not: Do not give the term for free — extract the price move first.

branches.if_you_hear: Counterparty offers bundle ('we can do A and B if you do X and Y').
branches.interpretation: They're playing the right game. Engage on bundle terms.
branches.say: Counter-bundle. Don't accept their bundle as-is — modify two pieces and re-offer.
branches.do_not: Do not pick at one part of their bundle while ignoring the rest. Engage with the whole.

branches.if_you_hear: After 2 rounds of multi-issue trading, both sides circling without close.
branches.interpretation: Either the gap is real OR there's an undisclosed constraint.
branches.say: Surface the constraint: 'What's the one thing that, if we solved it, would let you close today?'
branches.do_not: Do not keep cycling dimensions. The blocker is now meta, not dimensional.

branches.if_you_hear: Counterparty says 'I have to ask my manager about X' on a specific dimension.
branches.interpretation: That dimension is the one with the most authority headroom. Manager has more latitude there than on price.
branches.say: Press on that dimension: 'Great — while you check, can we also explore if there's more room on [Y]?' Manager-call is your moment.
branches.do_not: Do not focus only on price during the manager pause. The whole bundle gets reviewed.

boundaries: Do NOT trade dimensions the user has not authorized (term, exclusivity, scope changes).
Do NOT bundle in items the user has marked non-negotiable.
Do NOT invent dimensions to bluff with — if you mention 'autopay' you must be willing to do it.
Do NOT continue introducing dimensions past the user's complexity tolerance — some users want a simple deal, even if suboptimal.
success_signals: Counterparty asks YOU questions about your priorities — multi-issue dialogue is active.
Counterparty proposes asymmetric trades (their cheap-thing for your valuable-thing or vice versa).
Final deal includes 2+ non-price terms that wouldn't have been in a single-issue close.
Counterparty thanks you for being 'creative' or 'flexible' — both code for 'you played the right game'.
failure_signals: Counterparty firmly refuses to discuss any non-price dimension after 2 attempts.
Bundle proposed by you gets dismantled piece-by-piece — counterparty is cherry-picking.
You've introduced 4+ dimensions and the deal hasn't moved — too many open variables; consolidate.

exit_strategy.on_success: Confirm the FULL bundle in writing — every dimension, not just price. Use `commitment_check` and make sure each item has an owner and timing.
exit_strategy.on_failure: If multi-issue trading doesn't move the deal, the constraint is probably authority or fixed-pricing. Escalate or accept the single-issue outcome and consider walking (see `10-walkaway-threats`).

required_user_info: Full list of dimensions the user can trade (with authority levels for each).
User's ranking of which dimensions matter to THEM (so concessions are made on lower-ranked dimensions).
Any hypothesis about what matters most to the counterparty.
